narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 40.9% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is high enough to flag concerns based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile includes being in the bottom decile for Year 1 school grades, the fourth decile for Year 3 grades—both below the typical student's standing—along with an undergraduate GPA of 2.4, well under the average of 3.21, and a notably low LSAT score of 25 compared to the usual 36.7. You're from an upper-middle-income family, identify as a Hispanic male planning to study part-time, which puts you in some less common groups since most applicants are white, full-time, and from varied income levels. When the model weighed all this, your Year 3 academic decile stood out as the strongest factor nudging the prediction toward potential bar failure, followed closely by your Year 1 decile. Your family income level and LSAT score came next in influence, both contributing toward that concerning outcome, with your racial background and undergraduate GPA also playing supporting roles in the same direction. Your choice to go part-time added a bit more weight toward the risk, while being male was the one element that slightly favored a passing prediction, as it aligns with a majority of successful past students. Compared to typical applicants who pass the bar, your early and later school rankings lag significantly behind the middle-of-the-pack averages, and your test scores are in the lower range that often correlates with struggles ahead. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just the model highlighting patterns where students with similar academics faced higher bar hurdles. To boost your chances next time, prioritize strengthening your academic record through better grades or additional coursework to climb those deciles, and seriously consider retaking the LSAT to aim closer to or above the average. Switching to full-time study could also help, as it matches the profile of most bar passers. Keep building a strong story in your application, perhaps with extracurriculars or work experience that show resilience. You're already ahead by understanding this feedback—use it to pivot and reapply stronger. We believe in second chances and wish you the best in turning this around.
